title: Codimension two cycles
speaker: Madhav Nori
affiliation: University of Chicago; Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: Problems analogous to Bloch's conjecture, and some tentative methods of attacking them, will be discussed.

proof_stages.label: Framing: CH0 and generic-point cohomology
proof_stages.summary: The speaker fixes the base field as the complex numbers and frames the goal as understanding zero-cycles on surfaces and related Bloch-Ogus-type cohomology at the generic point.

proof_stages.label: Mumford-Roitman obstruction to finite-dimensional generation
proof_stages.summary: The Mumford-Roitman method assigns Kunneth components of coherent cycle classes to families of cycles, and a nonzero component obstructs generation of the Chow group in small dimension; applied to the diagonal, it gives infinite-dimensional CH0 when H^0(X,Omega_X^e) is nonzero for some e≥2.

proof_stages.label: Roitman Albanese control and non-effectivity
proof_stages.summary: Roitman's theorems identify torsion, and under finite-dimensionality all of CH0^0, with the Albanese variety, but these results still do not decide whether a particular zero-cycle or a member of a one-parameter family is rationally equivalent to zero.

proof_stages.label: Codimension-two Abel-Jacobi setup and conjectural kernels
proof_stages.summary: For codimension-two cycles the speaker recalls the cycle class map, Griffiths' Abel-Jacobi map to the intermediate Jacobian, and formulates Bloch-Beilinson-style conjectures identifying the relevant Abel-Jacobi or Deligne-type kernel with algebraic and then rational equivalence under suitable hypotheses.

proof_stages.label: Support-theoretic consequences of Beilinson-Hodge and Bloch-Ogus
proof_stages.summary: The conjectural package is related to known frameworks: the codimension-two conjecture would imply Bloch's conjecture for p_g=0 surfaces via a diagonal decomposition and nilpotence, while the Beilinson-Hodge-Jannsen conjecture plus the Bloch-Ogus theorem gives the algebraic-equivalence assertion in codimension two.

proof_stages.label: Kernel of Jacobians of curves on a surface
proof_stages.summary: Given curves on a surface, the kernel of the map from the sum of their Jacobians to CH0 and then to Albanese is decomposed into simple abelian factors, each represented by a family over a curve whose transverse Abel-Jacobi invariant detects nonvanishing of the associated zero-cycles outside a countable subset, with a converse predicted by Bloch-Beilinson.

proof_stages.label: Bloch-Ogus generic-point reformulation of CH0
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines H^q(K_X) as direct-limit cohomology of Zariski opens, analyzes it using Poincare duality and Deligne log complexes, and in degree two for a surface obtains a map theta to the cohomology of all curve normalizations whose cokernel fits into an exact sequence with CH0(X).

proof_stages.label: Pencil strategy and analytic-versus-algebraic obstruction
proof_stages.summary: A hoped-for fiberwise argument using a pencil on a surface would try to put a motivic/generic-point structure on H^2(X,O_X), but the needed exact sheaf sequence fails because algebraic or globally extendable sections do not surject onto arbitrary holomorphic local sections in the usual topology.

examples.name: Fixed-point zero-cycle locus on a surface with p_g nonzero
examples.note: For a fixed point p0 on a surface with p_g≠0, the locus of points x with [x-p0]=0 in CH0 is only known as a countable union of proper curves and points, illustrating the non-effective nature of the Mumford-Roitman infinitude theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: he fixes a point p0 on a surface with pg≠0, considers the locus of points x with [x]=[p0] in CH0, and analyzes it as a countable union of proper curves and points

passes.reason: the locus of points rationally equivalent to a fixed point is used as a thin but ordinary consequence of Mumford/Roitman, not as a failure case.
The locus is described as a thin countable union of curves and points inside the surface, not as a refutation object itself.

Mumford's positive-p_g surface makes the fixed-point CH_0 fiber only a countable union of proper subvarieties, obstructing the naive claim that all degree-zero point differences are rationally trivial.
Mumford's pg>0 surface locus is used to show that many degree-zero cycles x-p0 are not rationally trivial, but the locus is not structurally sporadic, symmetric, or extremal.
It is the rational-equivalence fiber of a fixed point in CH_0, a thin countable union of proper subvarieties but not a sporadic, symmetric, extremal, or collapsed object.

It is the thin locus of points rationally equivalent to a fixed point on a pg≠0 surface, used illustratively rather than as a sporadic, extremal, symmetric, or refuting object.

examples.name: Griffiths' homologically trivial codimension-two cycle
examples.note: The historical example of a homologically trivial cycle not forced into algebraic equivalence illustrates why the Abel-Jacobi invariant is needed beyond the ordinary cycle class.
examples.engagement_reason: he cites Griffiths’ first homologically trivial codimension-two cycle detected by the Abel-Jacobi map but gives no construction or computation of the cycle

passes.reason: Griffiths’ null-homologous codimension-two cycle is invoked to disprove the implication from homological triviality to algebraic torsion/triviality.
Griffiths’ cycle is used to disprove that homologically trivial codimension-two cycles must be algebraically trivial up to torsion.
Griffiths' cycle is used precisely to show that homological triviality need not imply algebraic triviality even up to nonzero multiples.

examples.name: The q=1 curve case and Abel's theorem
examples.note: For a curve, the generic-point cohomology construction recovers integration of differentials and the kernel of principal divisors, making the later q=2 surface construction concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: he runs the q=1 curve case through the cohomological setup, integrates global differentials, and identifies the kernel with principal divisors as Abel’s theorem

passes.reason: the q=1 curve situation is the minimal base case of the higher-cohomology discussion and serves as the standard Abel-theorem model.
The q=1 curve situation is the minimal first-cohomology/one-dimensional base case used as a positive model via Abel’s theorem.
The q=1 curve/Abel theorem case is the minimal base case where the kernel is principal divisors, used as a model rather than as a failure example.
The q=1 curve case is the minimal low-dimensional base case where Abel's theorem gives the model calculation, not an adversarial example.
